1990.assessment:
Pre-war Sierra Leone had severe fiscal problems. APC government ran large
deficits funded by monetary financing, leading to hyperinflation. Government
debt was high relative to tiny economy. External debt burden significant.
Unsustainable fiscal position pre-war.

sources.citation: IMF Sierra Leone historical; APC fiscal mismanagement

1991.assessment:
War beginning. Military spending exploding. Fiscal deficit widening. Revenue
declining as war disrupted economy. Unsustainable fiscal position worsening.

sources.citation: IMF Sierra Leone 1991; war fiscal shock

1992.assessment:
NPRC coup. NPRC attempted some fiscal adjustment but war costs dominated.
Fiscal collapse as traditional revenue bases disrupted by war.

sources.citation: IMF Sierra Leone 1992; NPRC fiscal

1993.assessment:
NPRC fiscal stabilization attempts with IMF. Some improvement in inflation
control. Fiscal deficit still large but some reduction. Unsustainable position.

sources.citation: IMF Sierra Leone 1993; NPRC stabilization

1994.assessment: Continued fiscal stress. War costs. Unsustainable fiscal position.

sources.citation: IMF Sierra Leone 1994

1995.assessment:
EO hired (expensive) adding to fiscal burden. War near Freetown. Fiscal
unsustainable. External debt accumulating.

sources.citation: IMF Sierra Leone 1995; EO cost

1996.assessment:
Elections and civilian government. IMF engagement. Some fiscal stabilization
efforts. But unsustainable debt and deficit position continued with war.

sources.citation: IMF Sierra Leone 1996; Kabbah; IMF engagement

1997.assessment:
AFRC coup. IMF and donor programs suspended. Revenue collapsed. Fiscal
collapse — junta unable to maintain normal government finances. External
debt in arrears.

sources.citation: IMF Sierra Leone 1997; AFRC coup; programs suspended

1998.assessment:
Kabbah restored. Some international aid resumed. IMF re-engagement. But war
continuing. Fiscal position very difficult. External debt burden enormous.
Unsustainable fiscal position.

sources.citation: IMF Sierra Leone 1998; Kabbah restored; re-engagement

1999.assessment:
Freetown massacre destroyed infrastructure. Fiscal position extremely
stressed. Lomé Accord. External debt in arrears. Unsustainable.

sources.citation: IMF Sierra Leone 1999; Freetown massacre; Lomé

2000.assessment:
UNAMSIL expanding, UK intervention. International community providing
substantial budget support. Fiscal position still very poor with war.
Unsustainable debt burden.

sources.citation: IMF Sierra Leone 2000; budget support

2001.assessment:
DDR process. International support. War winding down reducing military costs.
Fiscal position improving but still very poor. HIPC Initiative beginning
to address debt burden.

sources.citation: IMF Sierra Leone 2001; HIPC Initiative; DDR

2002.assessment:
War ended January 2002. HIPC debt relief beginning. International donor
support massive. Military spending declining. Revenue recovering. Fiscal
position still very poor but improving trajectory. HIPC completion began
addressing unsustainable debt.

sources.citation: IMF Sierra Leone 2002; war ended; HIPC; donor support

2003.assessment:
HIPC debt relief significantly reducing debt burden. International donor
support continuing. Fiscal position improving. Revenue recovering. Large
deficit but manageable with donor support. Unsustainable position improving
with HIPC.

sources.citation: IMF Sierra Leone 2003; HIPC debt relief

2004.assessment:
HIPC completion point reached in 2006. In 2004, HIPC process ongoing.
Debt relief reducing burden. IMF ECF program. Revenue improving. Moderate
deficit with donor support. High but declining debt/GDP.

sources.citation: IMF Sierra Leone 2004; HIPC process; ECF program

2005.assessment:
HIPC completion approaching 2006. Fiscal consolidation. Revenue improving
with economic recovery. Moderate deficit. Elevated debt declining with
HIPC relief.

sources.citation: IMF Sierra Leone 2005; HIPC approaching completion

2006.assessment:
HIPC completion point reached 2006. Debt significantly reduced through
multilateral debt relief. Fiscal position improved significantly. Manageable
deficit. Debt/GDP declining sharply with HIPC relief.

sources.citation: IMF Sierra Leone 2006; HIPC completion

2007.assessment:
Post-HIPC fiscal improvement. Koroma elected, new government. IMF program
continuing. Revenue improving with economic growth. Small manageable deficit.
Debt manageable.

sources.citation: IMF Sierra Leone 2007; post-HIPC; Koroma

2008.assessment:
Fiscal improvement continuing. Iron ore investment attracting FDI.
Revenue growing with economy. Manageable deficit. Debt/GDP declining.

sources.citation: IMF Sierra Leone 2008; iron ore investment

2009.assessment:
Global financial crisis. Revenue somewhat reduced. Deficit slightly elevated.
IMF program. Small to moderate deficit.

sources.citation: IMF Sierra Leone 2009; global crisis

2010.assessment:
Iron ore boom creating revenue. Fiscal position improving. Government
investing in infrastructure. Manageable deficit. Debt/GDP declining.

sources.citation: IMF Sierra Leone 2010; iron ore revenue

2011.assessment:
Iron ore boom revenue. Fiscal position improving. Government spending on
public services. Manageable deficit. Debt/GDP moderate.

sources.citation: IMF Sierra Leone 2011; iron ore boom

2012.assessment:
Peak iron ore mining. Revenue peak. Fiscal position manageable to good.
Pre-election spending pressures. Small deficit manageable.

sources.citation: IMF Sierra Leone 2012; peak iron ore revenue

2013.assessment:
Iron ore revenue peak. Good fiscal position. Government investing in
infrastructure and social services. Debt/GDP declining further.

sources.citation: IMF Sierra Leone 2013; iron ore peak

2014.assessment:
Ebola from July 2014. Emergency spending. Iron ore mines closing. Revenue

2015.assessment:
Full Ebola year. Mines closed. Revenue collapsed. International emergency

2016.assessment:
Post-Ebola recovery. Revenue recovering. Deficit still elevated but improving.

Fiscal pressure from pre-election spending. Mudslide emergency costs.

Bio elected. Free Quality Education program launched — major new fiscal

sources.citation: IMF Sierra Leone 2018; Bio; Free Quality Education; IMF suspended

2019.assessment:
IMF re-engagement with new ECF program. Fiscal consolidation efforts. FQE
pressure. Elevated deficit. Debt/GDP manageable but elevated.

sources.citation: IMF Sierra Leone 2019; ECF; fiscal consolidation

2020.assessment:
COVID emergency. IMF emergency financing (RFI). Fiscal deficit widening.
Large deficit with COVID emergency spending. Debt/GDP elevated.

sources.citation: IMF Sierra Leone 2020; COVID; RFI emergency

2021.assessment:
Post-COVID. Revenue recovering. IMF ECF. Fiscal consolidation. Elevated
deficit declining. Moderate deficit.

sources.citation: IMF Sierra Leone 2021; ECF; fiscal consolidation

2022.assessment:
Very high inflation (27%) eroding revenues in real terms. Fiscal stress.
Elevated deficit. August 2022 protests reflecting fiscal/economic mismanagement.
Moderate to elevated deficit.

sources.citation: IMF Sierra Leone 2022; high inflation; fiscal stress

2023.assessment:
Very high inflation (47.6%). IMF ECF program for fiscal adjustment. Leone
severely devalued. Large fiscal pressures. Revenue insufficient. Large deficit.
Unsustainable without IMF program.

sources.citation: IMF Sierra Leone 2023; 47.6% inflation; IMF ECF; leone devaluation

2024.assessment:
IMF program stabilizing. Inflation declining (28.6%). Fiscal consolidation.
Deficit elevated but declining. Moderate deficit improving.

sources.citation: IMF Sierra Leone 2024; program; inflation declining

2025.assessment:
Continued IMF program. Fiscal consolidation trajectory. Moderate deficit
declining. Estimated manageable with ongoing program.

sources.citation: IMF projection Sierra Leone 2025

1990.assessment:
Pre-war baseline. Sierra Leone was already one of the world's poorest countries
with GDP per capita around USD 220. APC/Momoh governance was corrupt with
an economy based on diamonds, rutile, iron ore, and bauxite. The mining
sector had largely been captured by political elites. RUF had not yet launched
its insurgency.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI, Sierra Leone 1990

1991.assessment:
RUF launched insurgency March 1991. First year of war devastated the economy.
Mining disrupted in eastern regions. GDP per capita approximately USD 184
as reported by World Bank WITS data. Significant decline from 1990 as war
disrupted economic activity.

sources.citation: World Bank WITS NY.GDP.PCAP.CD Sierra Leone 1991

1992.assessment:
War intensifying with NPRC coup April 1992. GDP per capita approximately USD 163
according to World Bank data. Diamond, rutile, and other mining severely
disrupted. NPRC coup added political disruption to war damage. Major decline.

sources.citation: World Bank WITS NY.GDP.PCAP.CD Sierra Leone 1992

1993.assessment:
Slight recovery in 1993 to approximately USD 185. NPRC government attempting
counteroffensives. Some mining activity continuing in government-held areas.
War ongoing with widespread violence.

sources.citation: World Bank WITS NY.GDP.PCAP.CD Sierra Leone 1993

1994.assessment:
GDP per capita approximately USD 219 in 1994. Some improvement despite ongoing
war. Mining sector partially recovering in some areas. Currency fluctuations
affecting current USD values. War in eastern and southern regions but west
and north partially functioning.

sources.citation: World Bank WITS NY.GDP.PCAP.CD Sierra Leone 1994

1995.assessment:
GDP per capita approximately USD 208. RUF reached Freetown outskirts early 1995.
Executive Outcomes deployed. Kono diamond fields contested. Economic activity
severely disrupted during crisis periods. EO eventually recovered Kono.

sources.citation: World Bank WITS NY.GDP.PCAP.CD Sierra Leone 1995

1996.assessment:
Elections and civilian government restored. Abidjan Peace Accord signed. Some
economic recovery with elections signaling stability. GDP per capita approximately
USD 220. Mining sector partially recovering. International confidence improving
with civilian government.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 1996

1997.assessment:
AFRC coup May 1997 devastated the economy. ECOMOG blockade, international
sanctions, Kabbah in exile. Mining virtually halted. International investment
fled. GDP per capita collapsed to approximately USD 175. One of worst economic
years.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 1997; AFRC coup; ECOMOG blockade

1998.assessment:
Kabbah restored February 1998 but RUF controlled most of country. Economy
devastated by continued war. Mining industry unable to operate in RUF-held
areas. GDP per capita approximately USD 175 — essentially unchanged from
1997 crisis.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 1998

1999.assessment:
Freetown massacre January 1999 destroyed much of Freetown's economy.
Lomé Accord July 1999. GDP per capita approximately USD 165 — lowest
during war. Economy essentially collapsed with infrastructure destroyed,
mining halted, and massive displacement.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 1999; Freetown massacre

2000.assessment:
Continued war. Lomé Accord collapsed. UK intervention. UNAMSIL expanding.
Economy still devastated. GDP per capita approximately USD 165 — near
war-period low. Some mining in government areas but most economy paralyzed.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 2000

2001.assessment:
UNAMSIL DDR proceeding. Violence declining. Some economic recovery as
displaced populations returned. International aid increasing. Mining
beginning to recover in areas stabilized by UNAMSIL. GDP per capita
approximately USD 180 — slight improvement.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 2001

2002.assessment:
War ended January 2002. Peace dividend beginning. Elections May 2002.
International aid flowing in. Displaced populations returning. Mining
beginning to recover. GDP per capita approximately USD 200 — beginning
of post-war recovery. Still far below pre-war potential.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 2002; war ended; peace dividend

2003.assessment:
Post-war recovery accelerating. International donor support. TRC and Special
Court operating. Returns of displaced people. Mining sector expanding.
GDP per capita approximately USD 225. Steady recovery.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 2003

2004.assessment:
Continued post-war recovery. Local elections. Mining sector developing.
GDP per capita approximately USD 250. Infrastructure reconstruction beginning.
Steady growth from post-war low.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 2004

2005.assessment:
Continued recovery. UNAMSIL exited, UNIOSIL established. Mining sector
growing. International investment increasing. GDP per capita approximately
USD 295. Strong growth trajectory.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 2005

2006.assessment:
Steady economic recovery. International investment in mining. Agriculture
recovering. Elections preparing. GDP per capita approximately USD 340.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 2006

2007.assessment:
First peaceful transfer of power. Koroma elected. Iron ore investment
beginning at Tonkolili and Marampa. GDP per capita approximately USD 380.
Economic confidence improving with democratic consolidation.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 2007; Koroma elected

2008.assessment:
Steady growth. Iron ore and diamond mining developing. Global financial
crisis somewhat affected commodity prices. GDP per capita approximately
USD 390. Koroma government consolidating economic reforms.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 2008

2009.assessment:
Global financial crisis impact. Commodity prices reduced. GDP per capita
approximately USD 370 — slight decline from 2008. Recovery resumed from
2010 as iron ore investment projects advanced.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 2009

2010.assessment:
Recovery accelerating. Iron ore mines beginning production. Marampa mine
opened. GDP per capita approximately USD 425. Strong growth trajectory
as extractive sector boom beginning.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 2010; Marampa mine

2011.assessment:
Iron ore boom. Tonkolili mine opened by African Minerals. GDP grew at
extraordinary rate. GDP per capita approximately USD 540 — major jump
from 2010. Sierra Leone among fastest growing economies globally this year.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 2011; Tonkolili mine

2012.assessment:
Peak iron ore production year. Sierra Leone's GDP grew at ~15% in 2012 —
among highest in world. GDP per capita approximately USD 680. Koroma
re-elected. Multiple large iron ore mines operating. Boom years.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 2012; ~15% GDP growth

2013.assessment:
Continued iron ore boom. Multiple mines at full capacity. GDP per capita
approximately USD 790 — period peak approaching. GDP growth ~20% in real
terms driven by iron ore. UNIPSIL completed mission. Strong economic performance.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 2013; ~20% growth; iron ore full capacity

2014.assessment:
Period peak before Ebola collapse. GDP per capita approximately USD 820 in early
2014. Ebola outbreak from July-August 2014 brought iron ore mines to suspend
operations. Ebola devastated the economy in second half of 2014. Full-year
GDP impact still significant.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 2014; Ebola from July 2014

2015.assessment:
Catastrophic economic contraction. Ebola epidemic throughout 2015 until
November. Iron ore mines closed throughout. Commodity price crash also hurt.
GDP contracted ~21% in real terms. GDP per capita approximately USD 490 —
massive decline from 2014 peak. Economy devastated by twin shocks of Ebola
and commodity price collapse.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 2015; ~21% GDP contraction; Ebola + commodity crash

2016.assessment:
Post-Ebola recovery beginning. Some iron ore mines partially restarting. GDP
per capita approximately USD 510 — slight improvement. Economy gradually
recovering but iron ore sector still weak due to low commodity prices.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 2016

2017.assessment:
Gradual recovery. Mudslide disaster August 2017. Iron ore mines partially
operating. Commodity prices somewhat improving. GDP per capita approximately
USD 535.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 2017; mudslide

2018.assessment:
Bio elected March 2018. Economy continuing to recover. Agriculture and
services growing. GDP per capita approximately USD 560. Free Quality
Education program launched — significant fiscal commitment.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 2018; Bio elected

2019.assessment:
Steady growth. Services and agriculture performing. GDP per capita approximately
USD 600. Still far below 2014 peak due to sustained commodity price weakness.
Free Quality Education fiscal pressure.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 2019

2020.assessment:
COVID-19 pandemic affected growth. However, iron ore sector recovering and
new extractive investments supporting GDP. World Bank data shows significant
improvement in current USD terms partly due to exchange rate and new mining
activity. GDP per capita approximately USD 845 in current USD terms.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 2020; COVID; iron ore recovering

2021.assessment:
Post-COVID recovery. Iron ore sector. Agriculture growing. GDP per capita
approximately USD 885. Close to 2014 nominal peak.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 2021

2022.assessment:
High inflation and currency depreciation reduced USD value. Protests over
cost of living. GDP per capita approximately USD 860. Real growth positive
but nominal USD slightly below 2021 due to leone depreciation.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 2022; inflation; protests

2023.assessment:
GDP per capita approximately USD 758 in 2023 (World Bank data). Continued
high inflation (47.6% in 2023) eroding purchasing power. Currency depreciation
reducing current USD value. Real growth positive but nominal decline in USD
terms. Economy under fiscal stress.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 2023; 47.6% inflation

2024.assessment:
GDP per capita approximately USD 806 in 2024 (World Bank estimate). Inflation
declining from 2023 peak. IMF program supporting stability. Real growth
continuing. Currency somewhat stabilizing.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 2024; IMF program

2025.assessment:
Estimated GDP per capita approximately USD 850 based on IMF/World Bank
projections. Continued recovery trajectory. IMF program. Inflation declining.

sources.citation: World Bank/IMF projection Sierra Leone 2025

1990.assessment:
Pre-war Sierra Leone experienced very high inflation from poor monetary policy
and fiscal deficits under the APC government. GDP deflator inflation peaked at
~128% in 1991. In 1990, inflation was already extremely high, estimated around
110% based on historical data. Leone was a weak currency.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI CPI Sierra Leone 1990

1991.assessment:
Peak hyperinflation year. GDP deflator inflation reached ~128% in 1991 as
war began. RUF insurgency combined with pre-existing fiscal deficits and
poor monetary management to drive prices to extraordinary levels. Leone
severely devalued.

sources.citation: World Bank GDP deflator data; Sierra Leone 1991

1992.assessment:
Inflation declining from 1991 peak but still very high at approximately 65%.
NPRC coup April 1992. War ongoing. Monetary chaos. Leone continued to depreciate
though rate of inflation somewhat reduced.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 1992; NPRC coup

1993.assessment:
Significant inflation reduction. NPRC government attempting fiscal stabilization.
Inflation declining to approximately 23%. Still elevated but major improvement
from hyperinflation peak. IMF engagement under NPRC.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 1993

1994.assessment:
Inflation approximately 25%. War continuing. NPRC fiscal policies. Elevated
inflation persisting due to war disruption and fiscal deficits.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 1994

1995.assessment:
Inflation approximately 26%. RUF near Freetown, EO deployed. Continued
elevated inflation from war disruption and fiscal pressures.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 1995

1996.assessment:
Inflation approximately 23%. Elections and civilian government. Abidjan Accord.
Some stabilization with civilian government and international engagement.
Elevated but declining.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 1996

1997.assessment:
AFRC coup May 1997. ECOMOG blockade and economic collapse paradoxically may

political crisis, possibly due to supply collapse and reduced monetary activity.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 1997; AFRC coup

1998.assessment:
Kabbah restored February 1998. Inflation spiked to approximately 36% as
ECOMOG blockade lifted and normal monetary activity resumed but war
continued. Supply disruptions from ongoing conflict driving prices.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 1998

1999.assessment:
Freetown massacre January 1999 destroyed much of Freetown's economy and supply.

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 1999; Freetown massacre

2000.assessment:
GDP deflator showed deflation of approximately -4% in 2000 (as noted in

created complex price dynamics.

sources.citation: World Bank GDP deflator Sierra Leone 2000

UNAMSIL DDR bringing stability. Inflation approximately 10% as economy

sources.citation: World Bank WDI Sierra Leone 2001

2002.assessment:
War ended January 2002. GDP deflator reached record low of approximately

sources.citation: World Bank GDP deflator Sierra Leone 2002; war ended

Post-war recovery. Inflation approximately 8% as economy normalizing.

1990.assessment:
Pre-war one-party APC state. Civil liberties severely restricted. No free press,
political opposition banned, arbitrary detention common. Freedom House rated
Sierra Leone as Not Free with civil liberties score ~6 (1=most free, 7=least free).

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 1990

1991.assessment:
War beginning. APC state repression combined with RUF insurgent violence.
Civil liberties severely restricted by both government repression and war.
Freedom House Not Free, score ~6.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 1991

1992.assessment:
NPRC military coup suspended constitution. No civil liberties protections.
Military rule by decree. Political parties banned. War worsening. Civil
liberties at their worst — approaching no civil liberties. FH score ~7.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 1992; NPRC coup

1993.assessment:
Continued NPRC military rule. War ongoing. No civil liberties protections.
Military governed by decree. No independent press. Arbitrary detention.
FH score ~7.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 1993

1994.assessment:
Continued NPRC military rule. War intensifying. RUF terror against civilians
compounding state repression. No civil liberties. FH score ~7.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 1994

1995.assessment:
NPRC under pressure to transition. War crisis — RUF near Freetown. EO
operations. No civil liberties. Elections beginning to be planned for 1996.
FH score ~7 continuing.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 1995

1996.assessment:
Significant improvement with multi-party elections February 1996. Constitution
reinstated. Political parties allowed. Some press freedom. However, ongoing
war significantly restricted civil liberties in conflict zones. FH improved
to ~5 (Partly Free territory). Still significant restrictions due to war.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 1996; elections; constitution

1997.assessment:
AFRC coup destroyed civil liberties entirely. Junta rule, constitution suspended,
Kabbah in exile. RUF/AFRC coalition terrorized civilians. No civil liberties.
FH score back to ~7 (worst).

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 1997; AFRC coup

1998.assessment:
Kabbah restored February 1998. Civil liberties partially restored in Freetown
and government areas. However, RUF controlled most of country with no rights
protections. War atrocities widespread. FH score ~6 (severe restrictions
across most of country).

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 1998; Kabbah restored

1999.assessment:
Freetown massacre January 1999 with mass atrocities. Lomé Accord July 1999
gave RUF political positions. Ongoing war and atrocities. Civil liberties
severely restricted nationally. FH ~6.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 1999; Freetown massacre; Lomé Accord

2000.assessment:
Continued war. UNAMSIL hostage crisis. UK intervention. Sankoh captured.
Ongoing atrocities by RUF. Civil liberties severely restricted. FH ~6.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 2000

2001.assessment:
UNAMSIL DDR bringing stability. Violence declining. Government extending reach.
Civil liberties improving. Still significant restrictions in conflict areas
but some improvement. FH improving toward ~5.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 2001; UNAMSIL DDR

2002.assessment:
War ended January 2002. Free elections May 2002. Significant improvement in
civil liberties. Press beginning to operate more freely. Political competition
allowed. TRC established. Still significant gaps — war trauma, destroyed
infrastructure. FH improved to ~4 (Partly Free).

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 2002; war ended; elections

2003.assessment:
Continued improvement. TRC operating. Special Court. Press freedom improving.
Political parties operating freely. Some government harassment of journalists
but improving trend. FH ~4 (Partly Free).

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 2003

2004.assessment:
Local elections held. Press freedom improving. Civil society active. Some
seditious libel laws used against journalists. FH ~4 (Partly Free).

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 2004

2005.assessment:
Continued partial freedom. Independent press operating. Opposition active.
Some state harassment of critics. FH ~4 (Partly Free) with improvements
toward FH ~3 possible.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 2005

2006.assessment:
Pre-election year. Civil liberties improving. Independent media operating.
Political competition. Some seditious libel law prosecutions. FH ~4 (Partly Free).

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 2006

2007 elections marked a civil liberties milestone. Peaceful competitive election,
first transfer of power. Media covered elections freely. Opposition operated
freely. FH improved to ~3 (moving toward Free). Significant improvement.

Koroma government. Civil liberties mostly respected. Independent media.

Continued partial freedom. Independent press. Political opposition free.

1990.assessment:
APC/Momoh one-party state. Ruling coalition relatively unified under Momoh
but based on patronage. Growing discontent in military over low pay and
deteriorating conditions. RUF forming under Sankoh but not yet active.
Minor tensions within ruling coalition but nominally functional.

sources.citation: Academic literature; news analysis; Sierra Leone 1990

1991.assessment:
RUF insurgency began, stressing the APC/Momoh government. Military increasingly
frustrated with corruption and lack of resources to fight the war. Significant
elite competition and management tensions. APC coalition fracturing under war
pressure.

sources.citation: Academic literature; Sierra Leone 1991

1992.assessment:
NPRC military coup April 1992 by junior officers overthrew Momoh. The coup
itself indicated open factional conflict within the security establishment.
New military junta (NPRC) was internally cohesive initially but faced internal
power struggles. Complete fragmentation of previous civilian coalition.

sources.citation: NPRC coup April 1992; academic literature

1993.assessment:
NPRC junta ruling. Internal junta tensions between Strasser and other members.
Military factionalism developing. 'Sobel' phenomenon undermined military
cohesion. War creating competing interests within security establishment.
Significant competition managed within junta.

sources.citation: Academic literature; NPRC factionalism

1994.assessment:
NPRC internally fragmented. Active power struggles between Strasser and rivals.
Sobel phenomenon reflecting military incoherence. Pressure to return to
civilian rule increasing. Active power struggles within ruling military coalition.

sources.citation: Academic literature; NPRC fragmentation

1995.assessment:
January 1996: Strasser overthrown internally by Julius Maada Bio (within NPRC).
In 1995, active power struggles within NPRC leading up to this. Elections
scheduled for 1996 under international pressure. Bio took power and agreed
to hold elections. Active power struggles.

sources.citation: NPRC internal coup Jan 1996 (Bio ousted Strasser)

1996.assessment:
Bio ousted Strasser January 1996 in internal NPRC coup. Bio then held elections
February 1996, transferring power to civilian Kabbah (SLPP). New civilian
coalition forming. Abidjan Peace Accord November 1996. Kabbah government
relatively cohesive but challenged by military/RUF threats. Significant
competition but managed under new civilian framework.

sources.citation: Bio ousted Strasser Jan 1996; Kabbah elected Feb 1996

1997.assessment:
AFRC coup May 1997 — complete elite fragmentation. Military split: AFRC/RUF
coalition vs. ECOMOG backing Kabbah in exile. Multiple competing authority
centers. Kabbah government in exile in Guinea. Complete fragmentation.

sources.citation: AFRC coup May 1997; Kabbah exile; multiple factions

1998.assessment:
Kabbah restored February 1998. Civilian government attempting to rebuild
coalition. Military unreliable — many AFRC sympathizers. CDF militia loyal
to Kabbah. Multiple competing groups. Significant elite fragmentation though
nominal civilian leadership restored.

sources.citation: Kabbah restored Feb 1998; military unreliable; CDF vs AFRC

1999.assessment:
Freetown massacre January 1999. Lomé Accord July 1999 gave RUF Sankoh
government position — integrating enemy into coalition. Multiple competing
groups: SLPP government, RUF (now with ministerial position), CDF, ECOMOG,
UNAMSIL. Elite cohesion extremely fragile. Active power struggles.

sources.citation: Lomé Accord July 1999; RUF in government; multiple factions

2000.assessment:
Lomé Accord collapsed. Sankoh captured May 2000. UK intervention. UNAMSIL
expanding. Multiple factions still contesting. Kabbah government backed by
UNAMSIL and UK. West Side Boys fractured government-aligned forces. Active
power struggles but Kabbah's position gradually strengthening.

sources.citation: Lomé collapse; Sankoh captured; UK intervention; West Side Boys

2001.assessment:
UNAMSIL DDR bringing stability. RUF disarming. Government coalition
consolidating. Kabbah building broader national support base ahead of
2002 elections. Significant competition but increasingly managed.

sources.citation: UNAMSIL DDR; Kabbah consolidating; 2002 elections

2002.assessment:
War ended January 2002. Kabbah re-elected May 2002 with 70% — massive
mandate. RUF got less than 2% of votes. SLPP won parliamentary majority.
Ruling coalition relatively stable and unified with strong popular mandate.
Minor tensions within SLPP but functional unified governance.

sources.citation: Kabbah 70% mandate; SLPP parliamentary majority; RUF less than 2%

2003.assessment:
Kabbah government post-war consolidation. TRC and Special Court creating
transitional justice framework. SLPP coalition relatively stable. APC as
loyal opposition. Military subordinate to civilian control. Stable unified
governance.

sources.citation: TRC; Special Court; SLPP consolidation; civilian control

2004.assessment:
Stable civilian government. SLPP consolidating. Local elections showing
multi-party competition. Military fully subordinate to civilian control.
Minor tensions but functional governance.

sources.citation: SLPP consolidation; civilian control; local elections

2005.assessment:
SLPP government preparing 2007 elections. Kabbah term-limited. SLPP
succession competition internally. APC building opposition. Stable governance
with competition managed within democratic framework.

sources.citation: SLPP succession planning; APC opposition; democratic competition

2006.assessment:
Pre-election year. APC and SLPP competing. Kabbah lame duck. Competition
intense but managed within democratic norms. Stable governance pending
2007 elections.

sources.citation: Pre-election competition; Kabbah lame duck

2007.assessment:
First peaceful transfer of power — APC's Koroma defeated SLPP. Both parties
accepted result. Koroma forming new government. SLPP in opposition. Elite
cohesion demonstrated through peaceful transfer. APC ruling coalition forming.
Stable unified new governance.

sources.citation: First peaceful transfer; Koroma forming government; SLPP opposition

2008.assessment:
Koroma APC government consolidated. Cabinet formed. Some internal APC
patronage tensions. SLPP in opposition. Military subordinate. Stable
unified governance with minor tensions.

sources.citation: Koroma consolidating; APC cabinet; SLPP opposition

2009.assessment:
APC government stable. Some APC-SLPP tensions around local elections and
opposition boycotts. Competition managed within democratic framework. Minor
tensions functional.

sources.citation: APC stable; APC/SLPP competition; local elections

2010.assessment:
Stable APC governance. Iron ore boom providing economic stability. Military

2011.assessment:
APC stable governance. High economic growth from iron ore. Election campaigns
beginning. Both APC and SLPP organizing. Competitive democracy functioning.
Minor tensions functional.

sources.citation: APC stable; iron ore; election campaigns

2012.assessment:
Koroma re-elected November 2012. SLPP conceded. Second peaceful election.
APC coalition stable with re-elected mandate. Minor tensions functional.
Stable unified governance.

2013.assessment:
APC government mid-term. Some corruption concerns. UNIPSIL completed mission.
Stable unified governance though patronage tensions ongoing.

sources.citation: UNIPSIL completed; APC mid-term; patronage concerns

2014.assessment:
Ebola crisis. Government responding with international help. Elite cohesion
relatively maintained around national emergency response. APC united in
crisis management. Minor tensions functional under Ebola emergency.

sources.citation: Ebola emergency; APC united in response

2015.assessment:
Post-Ebola recovery. APC government managing recovery. Commodity price crash

sources.citation: Post-Ebola recovery; APC managing; commodity crash

2016.assessment:
APC stable governance. Koroma term-limited for 2018. APC succession competition
beginning. SLPP organizing. Minor tensions functional with succession uncertainty.

2017.assessment:
APC internal succession competition. Samura Kamara chosen as APC presidential
candidate. Mudslide disaster. Competition managed within APC. SLPP building
around Bio. Significant competition but managed.

sources.citation: APC succession; Kamara candidate; mudslide disaster

2018.assessment:
Bio (SLPP) won March 2018. APC accepted defeat. Second peaceful transfer of
power. Bio forming SLPP government. APC in opposition. Elite cohesion through
democratic competition. New SLPP ruling coalition forming. Stable unified
governance.

sources.citation: Bio won; APC accepted; second transfer; SLPP forming government

2019.assessment:
Bio SLPP consolidating. APC-SLPP tensions intensified — APC boycotted local
elections. Executive-judiciary disputes emerging. SLPP coalition internally
managing minor tensions while facing opposition hostility. Significant competition
managed within government; APC openly hostile in opposition.

sources.citation: APC boycott; executive-judiciary disputes; SLPP consolidating

2020.assessment:
COVID emergency. SLPP government managing crisis. Some APC-SLPP tensions
around COVID measures. Government coalition managing under emergency. Minor
tensions functional within SLPP coalition.

sources.citation: COVID emergency; SLPP managing; APC/SLPP tensions

2021.assessment:
Bio SLPP consolidating. APC-SLPP tensions. Election preparation for 2023.
SLPP internally minor tensions. Significant competition managed.

sources.citation: Bio consolidating; 2023 election prep; APC/SLPP tensions

2022.assessment:
August 2022 protests reflected social discontent threatening to fracture SLPP
coalition support base. APC-SLPP tensions high. Government crackdown created
further tensions. Open factional conflict between ruling party and opposition.

sources.citation: Aug 2022 protests; APC/SLPP open conflict; crackdown

2023.assessment:
Bio re-elected in disputed June 2023 elections. APC refused to accept result.
November 2023 coup attempt — small group of plotters quickly suppressed.
Active power struggle signaled by coup attempt, though quickly defeated.
SLPP-APC open conflict over electoral legitimacy.

sources.citation: Disputed election; APC non-acceptance; coup attempt Nov 2023

2024.assessment:
Post-coup stabilization. Bio government consolidated. APC-SLPP tensions
ongoing but reduced from 2023 peak. Significant competition managed within
democratic framework after coup attempt failed.

sources.citation: Post-coup; Bio consolidated; APC/SLPP reduced tensions

2025.assessment:
Bio mid-term. APC-SLPP competition continuing. Significant competition managed
within framework. Economic stress.

sources.citation: ICG Sierra Leone 2025

1990.assessment:
One-party APC state under Momoh. Parliament existed but had no effective power.
Judiciary nominally independent but subject to executive pressure. Bureaucracy
deeply corrupt and dysfunctional. No free elections. Institutional functioning
severely degraded even before the war.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 1990; V-Dem

1991.assessment:
RUF insurgency began. APC/Momoh government struggling to respond. Military
increasingly taking independent action. Civil war severely strained already
weak institutions. State capacity declining rapidly in conflict zones.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 1991

1992.assessment:
NPRC military coup April 1992 suspended constitution, dissolved parliament,
banned political parties. Military junta ruled by decree. Courts remained
nominally functional but under military oversight. No elections, no legislature.
Institutions collapsed or nominal.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 1992; NPRC coup

1993.assessment:
NPRC junta continued. No parliament, no elections. Military governed by decree.
Some courts functioning in Freetown. War affecting government capacity in
conflict areas. Institutions remained collapsed or nominal.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 1993

1994.assessment:
NPRC junta continuing rule. Promised transition to democracy stalling. No
elections scheduled. War escalating. Institutions collapsed or nominal —
NPRC governing by decree, no functioning legislature.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 1994

1995.assessment:
NPRC government under pressure to return to civilian rule. Elections scheduled
for 1996. Executive Outcomes hired. War crisis near Freetown. Institutions
collapsed or nominal but transition process beginning.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 1995; transition planning

1996.assessment:
Elections held February 1996 — first multi-party elections since 1967.
Kabbah (SLPP) elected president. NPRC handed power to civilian government.
Parliament restored. Constitution reinstated. Institutions partially functional
— transition to democratic rule significant achievement despite ongoing war.
Abidjan Peace Accord signed November 1996.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 1996; Kabbah election; NPRC handover

1997.assessment:
AFRC coup May 1997 overthrew Kabbah's elected government. Constitution suspended
again, parliament dissolved, Kabbah fled to exile. AFRC/RUF junta governed by
terror. All democratic institutions collapsed. International community refused
recognition. Institutions completely collapsed.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 1997; AFRC coup May 1997

1998.assessment:
ECOMOG restored Kabbah February 1998. Civilian government reinstated, parliament
restored. However, RUF controlled most of the country. Institutions partially
functional in Freetown/western areas but completely absent in RUF-held areas.
Special Court for war crimes under discussion. Severely degraded across most
of the country.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 1998; Kabbah restored Feb 1998

1999.assessment:
Freetown massacre January 1999 devastated the capital. Lomé Accord July 1999
gave RUF political legitimacy including ministerial positions. Kabbah government
functioned nominally in Freetown. Parliament reconvened. However, RUF controlled
most of the country with no state institutions functioning. Severely degraded.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 1999; Lomé Accord July 1999

2000.assessment:
Lomé Accord collapsed. Kabbah government in Freetown. UNAMSIL expanding.
British intervention stabilized Freetown. Sankoh captured. Government institutions
functioning in Freetown but absent most of country. Severely degraded nationally.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 2000; UK intervention; UNAMSIL

2001.assessment:
UNAMSIL deployment and DDR process allowing government to extend authority.
Parliament functioning in Freetown. DDR process providing framework for
disarmament. Courts beginning to function more broadly. Institutions still
severely degraded but improving with UNAMSIL support.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 2001; UNAMSIL DDR; institutions recovering

2002.assessment:
War ended January 2002. Free and fair elections May 2002 — Kabbah re-elected
with 70%, SLPP won parliament. RUF political party received less than 2%.
Democratic institutions restored. Parliament functioning. Courts operating.
TRC established. Post-war institutional rebuilding beginning. Partially
functional — significant capacity gaps remain.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 2002; elections May 2002; TRC

2003.assessment:
Post-war institutional consolidation. TRC operating, Special Court for Sierra
Leone established. Parliament functioning. Courts operating with international
support. UNAMSIL present. Decentralization beginning — local councils being
re-established. Partially functional with ongoing capacity-building.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 2003; Special Court; decentralization

2004.assessment:
Continued institutional recovery. Local elections held for first time in decades.
Parliament functioning. Judiciary recovering. Police reform with international
support (SLSD). Civil service reform beginning. Partially functional with
significant ongoing capacity gaps.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 2004; local elections; police reform

2005.assessment:
UNAMSIL exited, replaced by UNIOSIL (advisory mission). Institutions increasingly
self-sustaining. Parliament functioning. Judiciary improving. Anti-Corruption
Commission established. Elections scheduled for 2007. Partially functional
with ongoing improvements.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 2005; UNIOSIL; ACC; judiciary

2006.assessment:
Continued institutional improvement. Election preparations. Parliament
functional. Judiciary improving but capacity gaps remain. Special Court
continuing trials. UNIOSIL supporting governance. Approaching functional
but flawed status.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 2006; UNIOSIL; Special Court

2007.assessment:
Landmark elections — first peaceful transfer of power. Institutions demonstrated
capacity to manage competitive elections. Independent Electoral Commission
functioned effectively. Parliament, courts, executive all functioning within
democratic norms. Functional but flawed — some patronage and corruption remain.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 2007; first peaceful transfer; IEC

2008.assessment:
Koroma APC government consolidating. UNIOSIL completing mission — confirmed
institutional sufficiency. Parliament, courts, executive functional. Some
executive overreach but within constitutional bounds. Functional but flawed.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 2008; UNIOSIL completion

2009.assessment:
Functional democratic institutions. Parliament operating. Judiciary functioning.
UNIPSIL providing advisory support. Elections scheduled for 2012. Some
corruption and patronage concerns but institutions operating within norms.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 2009; UNIPSIL

2010.assessment:
Functional democratic institutions. Parliament, courts, executive operating.
Iron ore boom attracting investment — governance challenges around resource
management. EITI compliant. Functional but flawed.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 2010; EITI compliant

2011.assessment:
Functional democratic institutions with high economic growth. Parliament operating.
Elections planned for 2012. Some concerns about executive power and APC patronage.
Functional but flawed.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 2011

2012.assessment:
Elections held November 2012 — credible elections, Koroma re-elected. Second
peaceful election. Parliament reconstituted. Functional democratic institutions
demonstrated robustness. SLPP conceded. Observers praised the process.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 2012; election observers

2013.assessment:
UNIPSIL completed mission — international confidence in institutional sufficiency.
No UN peacekeeping needed. Parliament, courts, executive operating. Functional
but flawed — corruption and patronage ongoing concerns.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 2013; UNIPSIL mission end

2014.assessment:
Ebola epidemic severely strained government capacity. State of emergency
declared. International organizations took major roles in response. Domestic
institutions partially overwhelmed but functioned. Parliament continued.
Partially functional due to Ebola emergency straining capacity.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 2014; Ebola epidemic

2015.assessment:
Ebola declining, Ebola-free November 2015. Government managing the recovery.
Institutions functioning though strained by economic contraction from Ebola.
Parliament, courts, executive operational. Functional but flawed.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 2015; Ebola-free

2016.assessment:
Post-Ebola recovery. Institutions functioning. Election preparation. Functional
but flawed with corruption concerns.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 2016

2017.assessment:
Pre-election year. Institutions operating. Some APC concerns about abuse of
incumbency advantage. Parliament functioning. Judiciary operating. Functional
but flawed.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 2017

2018.assessment:
March 2018 elections — second peaceful transfer of power, Bio (SLPP) won.
Institutions managed competitive elections successfully. IEC functioned.
Parliament reconstituted with SLPP majority. Functional democratic institutions
demonstrated resilience through another transfer of power.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 2018; second peaceful transfer

2019.assessment:
Bio SLPP consolidating. APC boycotts creating parliamentary dysfunction.
Executive-judiciary tensions emerging. Some concerns about executive overreach.
Institutions functioning but with partisan stress.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 2019; APC boycott; executive-judiciary tension

2020.assessment:
COVID-19 emergency measures including state of emergency. Parliament continued.
Courts functioning. Executive COVID response strained capacity. Functional
but flawed with emergency context.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 2020; COVID emergency

2021.assessment:
Post-COVID recovery. Institutions functioning. APC-SLPP tensions but within
constitutional framework. Functional but flawed.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 2021

2022.assessment:
August 2022 protests and security forces response raised rule of law questions.
Parliament functioning. Courts operating. Some concerns about executive overreach
in protest response. Functional but flawed with rights concerns.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 2022; August 2022 protests

2023.assessment:
Bio re-elected in disputed June 2023 elections — APC disputed results but
courts upheld. November 2023 coup attempt quickly suppressed — security
institutions functioned. Parliament reconstituted. Institutions demonstrated
resilience against coup attempt. Functional but flawed with electoral disputes.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 2023; disputed election; coup attempt

2024.assessment:
Post-coup stabilization. Bio government consolidated. Parliament and courts
functioning. Some executive-opposition tensions. Functional but flawed.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 2024

2025.assessment:
Institutions continuing to function. Bio mid-term. Economic stress. Functional
but flawed.

sources.citation: Freedom House Sierra Leone 2025

1990.assessment:
Pre-war Sierra Leone under APC/Momoh rule experienced low-level political
violence — state repression, enforced disappearances, and intimidation rather
than organized armed conflict. RUF was still forming abroad. Low estimate of
conflict-related deaths.

sources.citation: UCDP Sierra Leone pre-war data

1991.assessment:
RUF launched insurgency March 1991. First year of war brought significant violence
as RUF advanced through eastern Sierra Leone, massacring civilians in villages.
RSLMF counteroffensives also caused civilian casualties. Conservative estimates
suggest 1,000+ conflict deaths in 1991 as RUF established presence in Kailahun
and surrounding districts.

sources.citation: UCDP Sierra Leone 1991; RUF insurgency launch

1992.assessment:
War intensifying. RUF expanded operations across south and east. NPRC coup
April 1992 added political instability. Sobel phenomenon emerged — soldiers
looting and terrorizing civilians. Violence spread across multiple districts.
Civilian casualties escalating significantly. Estimates of 2,000+ deaths.

sources.citation: UCDP Sierra Leone 1992; NPRC coup; sobel phenomenon

1993.assessment:
Continued war with high civilian casualties. RUF attacks on villages widespread.
RSLMF operations brutal in counterinsurgency. Kono diamond district violence
high as multiple factions competed for mining revenues. Abductions, amputations,
and terror tactics used by RUF against civilians. NPRC government's human rights
record poor. Estimated 3,000+ deaths.

sources.citation: UCDP Sierra Leone 1993; RUF terror tactics; Kono violence

1994.assessment:
Violence escalating. RUF increasingly employing systematic terror against
civilians — amputations, burning villages, forced recruitment of child soldiers.
RSLMF increasingly unable to distinguish between civilians and combatants.
War crimes widespread. Estimated 4,000+ deaths. Massive displacement ongoing.

sources.citation: UCDP Sierra Leone 1994; RUF systematic terror; child soldier recruitment

1995.assessment:
Near-worst year to date. RUF advance to Freetown outskirts in early 1995.
Executive Outcomes PMC engaged — heavy fighting as EO pushed RUF back.
Kono retaken from RUF. High military deaths on all sides plus heavy civilian
casualties. CDF (Civil Defence Forces/Kamajors) emerged as pro-government militia.
Estimated 5,000+ deaths combining military and civilian casualties.

sources.citation: UCDP Sierra Leone 1995; EO engagement; RUF near Freetown; Kamajors

1996.assessment:
Partial reduction in violence due to Abidjan Peace Accord and Executive Outcomes
operations. However, elections in February 1996 were accompanied by RUF attacks
to disrupt voting — amputating voters' hands. Violence declined after EO operations
but remained significant. Abidjan Accord led to EO withdrawal which allowed RUF
to regroup. Estimated ~3,000 deaths.

sources.citation: UCDP Sierra Leone 1996; election amputations; Abidjan Accord; EO withdrawal

1997.assessment:
AFRC coup May 1997 brought junta rule and alliance with RUF. ECOMOG operations
against junta/RUF caused significant casualties. Junta rule involved widespread
looting, violence, and repression against civilians. ECOMOG bombing of Freetown
also caused civilian casualties. Estimated ~4,000 deaths combining junta violence,
ECOMOG operations, and residual conflict.

sources.citation: UCDP Sierra Leone 1997; AFRC coup; ECOMOG vs AFRC/RUF

1998.assessment:
February 1998: ECOMOG expelled AFRC/RUF from Freetown with heavy fighting and
civilian casualties. RUF launched revenge attacks in late 1998 as precursor to
January 1999 Freetown assault. Towns like Koidu attacked. RUF amputations and
massacres widespread. Estimated 5,000+ deaths in 1998.

sources.citation: UCDP Sierra Leone 1998; ECOMOG operation Feb 1998; RUF retaliation

1999.assessment:
One of the worst years. January 1999: RUF/AFRC invaded Freetown in Operation
No Living Thing — massacring 5,000-6,000 civilians in three weeks, mass rapes,
amputations, burning buildings. ECOMOG expelled them with further fighting.
Lomé Peace Accord (July 1999) achieved ceasefire but RUF retained territorial
control. Total deaths for 1999 estimated 10,000+ including Freetown massacre,
continued provincial violence, and ECOMOG combat operations.

sources.citation: UCDP Sierra Leone 1999; Freetown massacre Jan 1999 (5-6k); ECOMOG; Lomé Accord

2000.assessment:
Continued significant violence. Lomé Accord collapsed — RUF captured 500 UNAMSIL
peacekeepers. British Operation Palliser engaged RUF forces. Sankoh captured May
2000. Continued RUF violence in provinces. West Side Boys active. Estimated
~5,000 deaths combining RUF attacks, British/ECOMOG operations, and provincial
violence.

sources.citation: UCDP Sierra Leone 2000; UNAMSIL hostage crisis; UK Operation Palliser; Sankoh

2001.assessment:
Significant reduction in violence as UNAMSIL deployed fully and DDR began.
RUF compliance with disarmament patchy but improving. Violence in provinces
continuing but declining significantly. By end 2001, most areas stabilizing.
Estimated ~2,000 deaths — still significant but major decline from peak years.

sources.citation: UCDP Sierra Leone 2001; UNAMSIL DDR; violence declining

2002.assessment:
War officially ended January 18, 2002. RUF dissolved and disarmed. Violence
dropped dramatically. Residual low-level incidents during elections but no
organized armed conflict. Kabbah re-elected. Post-war reconstruction beginning.
Deaths dropped to minimal — mostly criminal violence.

sources.citation: UCDP Sierra Leone 2002; war end Jan 2002; elections May 2002

2003.assessment:
Post-war peace consolidating. No organized armed conflict. TRC operating.
Special Court for Sierra Leone prosecuting war crimes. UNAMSIL peacekeepers
present. Minimal residual violence.

sources.citation: UCDP Sierra Leone 2003; post-war peace; TRC; Special Court

2004.assessment:
Minimal violence. No organized armed groups active. Post-war reconstruction
proceeding. Political competition democratic. Mostly criminal violence.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2004

2005.assessment:
Minimal political violence. UNAMSIL exit completed. UNIOSIL established.
No organized armed threats.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2005

2006.assessment:
Minimal political violence. Pre-election period with some inter-party tensions
but no significant violence. Special Court trials proceeding.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2006

2007.assessment:
Elections brought localized inter-party clashes between APC and SLPP supporters.
Overall violence low. First peaceful transfer of power achieved. Post-election
period stable.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2007; election clashes; first peaceful transfer

2008.assessment:
Post-election normalization. Minimal political violence. Koroma consolidating.
No organized armed groups.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2008

2009.assessment:
Minimal political violence. Some APC-SLPP inter-party clashes but low level.
No organized armed groups.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2009

2010.assessment: Minimal political violence. Stable governance. Iron ore boom. No armed threats.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2010

2011.assessment: Minimal political violence. Iron ore mining boom. No organized armed groups.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2011

2012.assessment:
Election year. Some localized inter-party violence during campaigns but overall
peaceful. Koroma re-elected. SLPP conceded.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2012; Koroma re-election

2013.assessment: Minimal political violence. UNIPSIL completed mission. No armed groups.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2013

2014.assessment:
Ebola outbreak. Some unrest related to quarantine enforcement. No organized
armed conflict. Ebola deaths not counted as political violence.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2014; Ebola

2015.assessment: Ebola declining. Minimal political violence. Ebola-free November 2015.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2015

2016.assessment: Minimal political violence. Post-Ebola recovery. No armed groups.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2016

2017.assessment:
August 2017 mudslide near Freetown killed 1,000+ civilians — natural disaster
not political violence. Some pre-election tensions. Minimal political violence.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2017; mudslide disaster

2018.assessment:
Election year. Some pre- and post-election violence between APC and SLPP.
Bio won in runoff. Second peaceful transfer of power despite campaign tensions.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2018; election; Bio elected

2019.assessment:
Post-election political tensions. APC boycotted local elections. Minimal
political violence. Bio government consolidating.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2019

2020.assessment: COVID emergency measures. Minor protests. Minimal political violence.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2020

2021.assessment: Minimal political violence. APC-SLPP tensions ongoing. No armed groups.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2021

2022.assessment:
August 2022 anti-government protests over high cost of living escalated into
violence. Security forces fired on protesters — at least 6 people killed.
Dozens arrested. Political violence highest since post-war period but still
moderate.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2022; August 2022 protests; 6 killed

2023.assessment:
Disputed June 2023 elections. Bio re-elected; APC claimed fraud. November 2023
coup attempt quickly suppressed. Several deaths in coup suppression. Low overall
political violence but coup attempt notable.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2023; disputed elections; coup attempt Nov 2023

2024.assessment: Post-coup stabilization. Minimal political violence. Bio government consolidated.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2024

2025.assessment: Minimal political violence. Bio mid-term. No armed groups.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2025

1990.assessment:
Sierra Leone in 1990 was under the one-party APC rule of President Momoh,
succeeding Siaka Stevens. The state had effective control over most territory
though governance was weak and corrupt. The economy was in deep crisis but no
active insurgency had begun. RUF was forming in Libya with Foday Sankoh but
had not yet launched its campaign. Pre-war baseline with nominal full control.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone pre-war data; academic literature

1991.assessment:
RUF launched its insurgency from Liberia on 23 March 1991, entering from the
Kailahun District in the east. By end of 1991, the RUF had seized significant
areas of Kailahun and Pujehun districts in the south-east. Government forces
(RSLMF) and ECOMOG (Nigerian-led) held most of the country but eastern border
regions were contested. Approximately 25% of territory was under challenge.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 1991 data; Uppsala UCDP

1992.assessment:
RUF expanded operations into multiple districts in the south and east. April 1992
military coup by NPRC (National Provisional Ruling Council) under Captain Strasser
overthrew Momoh. The coup was partly driven by military frustration with the war.
RUF controlled significant parts of Kailahun, Kenema, Pujehun and Bo districts.
Government forces held Freetown, major towns, and western regions. Approximately
35% of territory contested or under RUF influence.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 1992; NPRC coup April 1992

1993.assessment:
RUF continued expansion despite NPRC government's counteroffensive. RSLMF
pushed RUF back in some areas temporarily but could not hold gains. RUF
adopted guerrilla tactics with attacks on villages, mining areas, and supply
routes. The diamond-rich Kono district increasingly targeted. Government
controlled major towns and Freetown but rural areas of the south and east
remained contested. Approximately 40% contested or outside control.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 1993; Uppsala UCDP

1994.assessment:
RUF expanded its area of operation. The RSLMF increasingly unreliable — soldiers
acting as 'sobels' (soldiers by day, rebels by night), looting and attacking
civilians. The Executive Outcomes (South African PMC) hired in 1995 but in 1994
the situation continued to deteriorate. Large portions of the south, east, and
north were affected by RUF raids, though RUF did not hold territory consistently.
Kono diamond fields repeatedly changed hands.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 1994; 'sobel' phenomenon; academic literature

1995.assessment:
RUF advanced to within 20km of Freetown in early 1995, its closest approach to
the capital. Executive Outcomes (South African PMC) deployed and pushed RUF back
from the capital and Kono diamond fields. By year-end, the government had recovered
Kono and secured approaches to Freetown. However, RUF maintained extensive presence
in rural east and south. Approximately half of territorial control was contested
though population centers were largely recovered.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 1995; Executive Outcomes deployment; RUF advance to 20km from Freetown

1996.assessment:
Elections held in February 1996 — Ahmed Tejan Kabbah won presidency despite RUF
threats. NPRC handed power to civilian government. Executive Outcomes continued
operations and held RUF at bay. Abidjan Peace Accord signed November 1996 between
government and RUF, leading to EO withdrawal. Kono and major towns under government
control. Eastern and southern rural areas partially contested. Moderate improvement
from 1995 due to EO operations.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 1996; Abidjan Peace Accord Nov 1996; Kabbah election Feb 1996

1997.assessment:
May 1997 military coup by AFRC (Armed Forces Revolutionary Council) under Major
Koroma overthrew Kabbah, who fled to Guinea. RUF immediately allied with AFRC junta
and jointly held most of the country. ECOMOG (Nigerian-led) blockaded Freetown but
the AFRC/RUF coalition controlled the capital and most of the country. Kabbah
government operated in exile from Conakry. Most effective state control collapsed.
ECOMOG maintained a naval blockade. One of the worst years for territorial control.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 1997; AFRC coup May 1997; RUF-AFRC coalition

1998.assessment:
February 1998: ECOMOG forces expelled AFRC/RUF from Freetown and reinstated
Kabbah. However, RUF regrouped in the provinces and launched a massive offensive.
By end of 1998, rebels controlled over half the country including diamond-rich
areas. The government held Freetown and the western region but large swathes of
the north, east, and south were under RUF control. The security situation was
highly volatile with territory changing hands frequently.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 1998; ECOMOG reinstatement Feb 1998; RUF offensive

1999.assessment:
January 1999: RUF/AFRC invaded Freetown — Operation No Living Thing — massacring
thousands of civilians (estimates 5,000-6,000 killed in 3 weeks). ECOMOG expelled
them from Freetown. Lomé Peace Accord signed July 1999 — gave RUF ministerial
positions and Sankoh the diamond mines portfolio. By mid-1999, RUF controlled
approximately 60-70% of the country. Lomé Accord gave legitimacy but RUF maintained
territorial hold. Freetown and western peninsula were the only secure areas.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 1999; Freetown assault Jan 1999; Lomé Peace Accord July 1999

2000.assessment:
Lomé Accord collapsed — RUF captured 500 UNAMSIL (UN) peacekeepers in May 2000.
Britain intervened (Operation Palliser) to evacuate citizens and stabilize Freetown.
Sankoh captured May 2000. UNAMSIL mandate strengthened. However, RUF still controlled
large areas. By end 2000, UNAMSIL and CDF forces gradually pushing RUF back in some
areas. Government controlled western areas and major towns; RUF held north and east.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2000; UNAMSIL hostage crisis May 2000; Operation Palliser; Sankoh capture

2001.assessment:
UNAMSIL (17,500 troops) progressively deployed and pushed RUF out of major
areas. RUF began disarming under the DDR process. By end 2001, UNAMSIL had
deployed throughout most of the country. Government control re-established
over most towns and main routes. RUF still present in some rural areas but
losing territorial hold. Significant improvement from 2000. The disarmament
process was proceeding.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2001; UNAMSIL deployment; DDR process; RUF disarmament

2002.assessment:
War officially ended January 18, 2002 — President Kabbah declared the civil war
over. RUF formally dissolved and disarmed. Free and fair elections held May 2002 —
Kabbah won in a landslide with 70% of the vote; SLPP won parliamentary majority.
RUF's political party received less than 2% of votes. UNAMSIL maintained 17,500
troops. Government authority re-established throughout most of the country though
some capacity gaps remained in remote areas. Near-full territorial control restored.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2002; war end Jan 2002; Kabbah re-elected May 2002

2003.assessment:
Post-war consolidation. Government authority steadily expanding throughout the
country. UNAMSIL peacekeepers gradually drawing down. TRC (Truth and Reconciliation
Commission) operating. Special Court for Sierra Leone established. No significant
armed challenges to state authority. Residual disarmament continuing. Economic
recovery beginning with international donor support. Full state control extending
to previously RUF-held areas.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2003; TRC; Special Court for Sierra Leone

2004.assessment:
Continued post-war recovery. Government authority firmly established nationwide.
UNAMSIL transitioning toward exit. Sierra Leone Police reforming with international
support. Local elections held for the first time in decades. No armed challenges
to state authority. EITI candidacy sought. Full control with ongoing capacity
building challenges in remote areas.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2004; UNAMSIL transition; local elections

2005.assessment:
UNAMSIL completed withdrawal and was replaced by UNIOSIL (UN Integrated Office).
Government authority consolidated nationwide. No armed threats to state control.
SLPP government preparing for 2007 elections. Mining sector recovering with iron
ore and diamond exports. Full territorial control maintained. Capacity gaps in
service delivery but no territorial challenges.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2005; UNAMSIL exit; UNIOSIL established

2006.assessment:
Full government control throughout the country. No armed opposition. Special Court
for Sierra Leone continuing trials of war crimes perpetrators. UNIOSIL supporting
governance capacity. Economic growth accelerating. EITI candidate status. Political
parties organizing for 2007 elections. Full territorial control.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2006; Special Court trials; UNIOSIL

2007.assessment:
Landmark democratic milestone: 2007 presidential and parliamentary elections
resulted in the first peaceful transfer of power in Sierra Leone's history.
Ernest Bai Koroma (APC) defeated SLPP in a runoff. The transfer was peaceful
and internationally acclaimed. Full government control throughout the country.
No armed threats to state authority. Democratic consolidation proceeding.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2007; Koroma election; first peaceful transfer of power

2008.assessment:
Koroma APC government consolidating power. Full state authority throughout the
country. UNIOSIL mission completed December 2008; replaced by UNIPSIL. Economic
recovery strengthening with iron ore and diamond mining. No armed challenges to
state authority. Minor political tensions between APC and SLPP but within
democratic norms.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2008; UNIOSIL to UNIPSIL transition

2009.assessment:
Full government control maintained. Inter-party political tensions between APC
and SLPP occasionally flared into localized violence (not armed insurgency).
EITI compliant status pursued. Iron ore sector attracting major investment
(African Minerals, London Mining). Economy growing. No threats to territorial
integrity.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2009; APC/SLPP tensions; EITI pursuit

2010.assessment:
Full government control. UNIPSIL mandate ongoing. Iron ore sector beginning
production at Marampa and Tonkolili mines. EITI compliant. No armed threats.
Preparations for 2012 elections beginning. Stable governance under Koroma.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2010; UNIPSIL; iron ore mines

2011.assessment:
Full territorial control. Iron ore mining boom (Tonkolili mine opened). GDP
growth among highest in world due to extractive sector. UNIPSIL mission active.
No armed challenges. Political competition between APC and SLPP peaceful.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2011; Tonkolili iron ore mine opened

2012.assessment:
Presidential and parliamentary elections held November 2012 — Koroma re-elected
with 58.7% in first round. Second consecutive peaceful election. SLPP conceded
results. Full state authority throughout the country. Iron ore and diamond sector
at peak production. UNIPSIL completed its mission December 2013 but in 2012
still active.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2012; Koroma re-elected Nov 2012

2013.assessment:
Full territorial control. UNIPSIL completed mission December 2013 — signifying
international confidence in Sierra Leone's stability. No UN peacekeeping mission
required. Economy performing strongly. Iron ore and diamond exports high. Stable
democratic governance.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2013; UNIPSIL mission completion

2014.assessment:
Ebola virus disease epidemic declared July-August 2014, devastating the country.
State of emergency declared. International community responded with massive
support. Movement restrictions and quarantine zones affected governance capacity.
However, no armed challenges — the state remained nominally in full control though
administrative capacity strained by the health emergency. Iron ore mining disrupted.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2014; Ebola epidemic state of emergency

2015.assessment:
Ebola epidemic continued through much of 2015 — Sierra Leone declared Ebola-free
November 2015. Economy severely contracted with iron ore mines closed. Full state
control maintained. International community provided extensive support. Commodity
price crash also devastated iron ore exports. Recovery planning beginning.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2015; Ebola-free Nov 2015; iron ore mines closed

2016.assessment:
Post-Ebola recovery. Economy beginning to recover. Iron ore sector partially
restarting. Full state control maintained. APC preparing for 2018 elections —
Koroma not eligible for third term. Stable governance, no armed threats.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2016; post-Ebola recovery

2017.assessment:
August 2017 mudslides disaster killed over 1,000 people near Freetown — state
emergency response mounted. Full territorial control. Election campaigning began.
APC and SLPP competing democratically. No armed challenges. Economy gradually
recovering from Ebola and commodity price shocks.

sources.citation: ACLED Sierra Leone 2017; August 2017 mudslide disaster

2018.assessment:
March 2018 elections — Julius Maada Bio (SLPP) defeated APC candidate in runoff.
Second peaceful transfer of power between parties. International observers declared
elections credible. Full state control. Democratic consolidation continuing. New
government launched Free Quality Education program.
